rationale: The citing paper uses Nishikawa et al. (2017) to support a specific prior finding: labeling products as crowd-involved can improve nonparticipating consumers' evaluations (e.g., innovation and need-fit in crowdsourcing contexts). It then explicitly positions its own contribution against that crowd-based stream, arguing that crowdfunding signals may differ and reporting a follow-up comparison where crowdfunding outperforms crowdsourcing in a technical category. This is more than a passing background mention because the cited work helps frame the paper's comparative theoretical positioning.
relation_description: The citing paper extends the logic of prior crowdsourcing-label effects (including Nishikawa et al. 2017) to a different signal domain: financing source (crowdfunded) rather than ideation source (crowdsourced). It uses the cited work as a conceptual benchmark and then contrasts boundary performance of crowdfunding versus crowdsourcing.
